explicit_text: I joined the United States Marine Corps Reserve back in March of 1950 while I was still in high school. Honestly, I joined mainly to play basketball with the Marine Corps Reserve team since I wasn't good enough for my high school team. A few of us from my class joined together, and shortly after, our unit was activated for the Korean War in July 1950. It was a big change going from reserve status to active duty, but it was a defining moment for me.
implicit_text: I joined the Marine Corps Reserve back in March of 1950 while I was still in high school. Honestly, I joined mainly to play basketball with the Marine Corps Reserve team since I wasn't good enough for my high school team. A few of us from my class joined together, and shortly after, our unit was activated for the conflict in Korea in July 1950. It was a big change going from reserve status to active duty, but it was a defining moment for me.

explicit_text: The Korean War started in June 1950, just a few months after I joined the Marine Corps Reserve. Our unit was called to active duty around mid-July 1950, and I was just 17 years old. I spent 11 months in Korea, mostly on the east central front, which was very mountainous. We faced harsh winters, fought in tough terrain, and endured long periods on the front lines, including holidays like Thanksgiving and Christmas. It was a challenging time, but I was fortunate to come home without being wounded.
implicit_text: The conflict in Korea started in June 1950, just a few months after I joined the Marine Corps Reserve. Our unit was called to active duty around mid-July 1950, and I was just 17 years old. I spent 11 months in Korea, mostly on the east central front, which was very mountainous. We faced harsh winters, fought in tough terrain, and endured long periods on the front lines, including holidays like Thanksgiving and Christmas. It was a challenging time, but I was fortunate to come home without being wounded.

explicit_text: When I first trained for Korea, I was part of a heavy weapons company specializing in the 3.5-inch rocket launcher, which was an anti-tank weapon. However, when we arrived in Korea, there were virtually no enemy tanks, so we quickly switched to machine guns. I only fired the 3.5-inch rockets three times, all in training for familiarization, never in actual combat. Despite this, we always carried the rockets with us, but they were never used in battle.

However, when we arrived in Korea, there were virtually no enemy tanks, so we quickly switched to machine guns. I only fired those rockets three times, all in training for familiarization, never in actual combat. Despite this, we always carried the rockets with us, but they were never used in battle.

explicit_text: Towards the end of my time in Korea, the peace talks were going on at Panmunjom, and our front lines became static. We dug in on a ridge and stayed put through the winter, conducting patrols and defensive actions. It was a tough time, with freezing cold weather and constant threat from enemy fire, but the ceasefire talks at Panmunjom meant the fighting was winding down. We stayed vigilant, but the war was essentially at a stalemate during that period.
implicit_text: Towards the end of my time in Korea, the peace talks were going on at the truce village, and our front lines became static. We dug in on a ridge and stayed put through the winter, conducting patrols and defensive actions. It was a tough time, with freezing cold weather and constant threat from enemy fire, but the ceasefire talks at that location meant the fighting was winding down. We stayed vigilant, but the war was essentially at a stalemate during that period.

explicit_text: After spending the holidays and much of the winter on the front lines, our Marine unit was relieved by United States Army troops in late January. It was a relief to be pulled back after months of harsh conditions and constant danger. The Army took over the positions we had held on the ridge, and we finally got a break from the front-line duty. I remember seeing the Army soldiers come in just as winter was starting to ease up.
implicit_text: After spending the holidays and much of the winter on the front lines, our Marine unit was relieved by Army troops in late January. It was a relief to be pulled back after months of harsh conditions and constant danger. The Army took over the positions we had held on the ridge, and we finally got a break from the front-line duty. I remember seeing the Army soldiers come in just as winter was starting to ease up.

explicit_text: We faced the Chinese People's Volunteer Army on the Korean front, and they were very skilled, especially with their mortar fire. They used 120 mm mortars effectively, and their accuracy was frightening, often calculated with mathematics to hit right on target. Their mortar attacks were relentless, and many of our casualties came from their shelling. They were a formidable enemy, and their tactics kept us on edge throughout the war.
implicit_text: We faced the Chinese volunteer forces on the Korean front, and they were very skilled, especially with their mortar fire. They used 120 mm mortars effectively, and their accuracy was frightening, often calculated with mathematics to hit right on target. Their mortar attacks were relentless, and many of our casualties came from their shelling. They were a formidable enemy, and their tactics kept us on edge throughout the war.

explicit_text: The 120 mm mortar was a terrifying weapon we faced in Korea, especially from the Chinese forces. It's about two to two and a half feet long and nearly four inches in diameter, fired at a high angle to hit targets behind cover. The Chinese were experts at using these mortars, often landing rounds right in our positions. I remember one Thanksgiving when a mortar round hit a tent where some of my squad were eating, but luckily none of my men were inside at the time. Those mortars were deadly and kept us constantly alert.
